summary: Stancil argues that while data helps, it is not a decisive competitive advantage against stronger talent, intuition, or speed—especially in early-stage companies. Data’s real value is a small, compounding edge across many decisions, not dramatic breakthroughs on a few big bets.
money_quotes: Great data teams likely make somewhat better guesses, but at the end of the day, we’re all still gambling.
Data offers the same promise. Its constant presence in an organization is like knowing the count of the deck.
Though it makes us a bit more informed in each decision, the effect is only felt in the aggregate, as the small edge compounds over time.
Being data-driven is a long game. It takes time for an advantage to accumulate.
Data is a competitive edge, but it’s earned, from months and years of grinding at the table, counting cards, playing hand after hand, slowly bringing down the house.
key_insight: Data-driven companies can win, but only because steady, modest advantages compound over many decisions rather than because data guarantees better outcomes on big strategic bets.
